item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don’t understand complicated equations like, from calculus to complex analysis but, I like it!

(Someone teach me, lol.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
I like it, and for three large reasons

+ show block
– hide block

1. It’s an example of “mundane made magical”, the thing is just a signal amplifier and that’s what it’s meant to be but it works on anomalous principals which make it work way better than any mundane signal amplifier ever could, along with all its other strange effects that probably weren’t intentional but are simply the result of its anomalous makeup. Showing how playing with the supernatural can have strange side effects. This exactly what Prometheus labs is all about. One the the things they do is try to create anomalous versions of mundane products, like the ghost bulbs, but just with many other of there products, they end up having some severe unintended side effects due to not fully understanding there anomalous workings

2. I like it when scps have there own “science” behind them. It shows that not everything anomalous is completely unexplainable it just works on principles that are different that the law says of physics, effectively just making it s different form of science, hence parascience. Plus when scps kind of have there own “science” about them it adds an extra dimension to there detail and “character” as it allows the reader to get a better feel as to the scps nature and inner workings

3. It’s an example of how not every scp has to be some kind of deadly monster or world ending object. It just has to be something that is anomalous and is sufficiently unusual as to warrant containment and a unique classification. This scp is just that, it’s nothing more than an anomalous amplifier but it’d the strangeness of its workings and the more dangerous of its effects that warrant scp classification

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the memetic model is valid, then this article is made of memes, by definition, because it carries semantic information. These equations do in fact check out mathematically (the simulation is, as described, accurately modeling the math). They aren't supposed to be, in and of themselves, anomalous if that is what you are asking though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These calculations make my head hurt, is this memetic or just complex?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This skip has several issues:

  1. The math actually hurts the article
  2. You include too much unimportant, boring information
  3. It's way too long for what it's worth
  4. The object itself is a remix of many tropes executed in an uninteresting way.
Also, the unknown graphic icon is not unknown at all
It actually is Argentina's 1978 World Cup Logo

I recommend a total rewrite that focuses on brevity, or that at least removes the unnecesary math.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lucky incubus/succubus, horrendously angry/unlucky everything else. Imagine Beelzebub getting trapped in it :P

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so previously on the wikiwalk, SCP-1576 was linked to this article, as both were acoustic devices powered by demons/spirits as the case would be, both having ties to Prometheus Labs. Seems fitting to link back to that skip here, as well as add a link to the PL hub at the small nod they are given. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

There are no IC manufacturers that have been known to use the manufacturer code "PL", and a "LM21D67" chip is not known to have been produced at any time by any manufacturer.

To this:

There are no IC manufacturers that have been known to use the manufacturer code "PL", and a "LM21D67" chip is not known to have been produced at any time by any manufacturer.

Changing this:

Scanning electron microscopy imaging has revealed unusual structures on the silicon IC die inconsistent with any known theory of operation.

To this:

Scanning electron microscopy imaging has revealed unusual structures on the silicon IC die inconsistent with any known theory of operation1.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similar markings are likewise noted in the schematics recovered for SCP-1576.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have added an interactive simulation of the effects. Its under the '+ show affected area simulation' collapsible at the end of the description of the AoE.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I upvoted because of the photo of the Gauchino 78, before I even read the article…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While in general I really like the technicality of it all:

  • If you're going to use a function like that, you probably want a graph or something. I wouldn't normally ask for a picture, but in this case having a contour plot or even just a "by the way, this is basically what the symbols mean" would help.
  • It's also not a particularly intuitive PDF for having the thing figure out how to pick songs. For one, there is no IGIS for an album. For another, the "damping" effect vaguely suggests that albums in the vicinity cast a decent "shadow" that I would expect to be mentioned more explicitly. Plus, songs are discrete, while this function is continuous, so where do you sample (or how do you integrate) the function to get the probability? It's not just a normalization issue.
  • The Ackermann function to measure a psychological phenomenon? I think it's overkill? Once the volume knob hits a 3, you're already hitting exponential growth of the input sound, which would be hard to distinguish from any of a number of other functions that perceptually "go off a cliff". It seems dubious that you could precisely measure anything beyond that, much less up to 4.5. For that matter, a really loud noise at setting 4 would be pretty much outside of human experience. It's not so much painful as not a thing that the human brain is equipped to register at all, making this one of the few musical SCPs where it would be more believable if this thing caused some kind of brain damage than if it just sounded really crazy.

I like the mathematical side of things, but it may have been trying too hard on that end, without providing details that really help to explain this thing (or to explain why it's mysterious, as the case may be). IMO, if you go too far towards making an SCP into an engineering problem, it gets more dull because the only two things you can do are to either ignore the technical material (which means it just gets in the way), or doggedly think through the implications until you realize that it makes no sense (because the real world doesn't work like that). There are some good reasons to hand-wave rather than get into the minutiae?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly. That is precisely the idea that is supposed to convey. Although as long as you don't turn the knob over around 4.5 it remains "manageable".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
jesus, an ackerman function?

It doesn't even make sense how loud that would be. 10 would be pretty much brain-melting, agony-inducing horror wouldn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to see more of what you have in store for this demonics thing. In the context of that, I'll probably upvote this, but until then I'm neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New headcanon accepted, poor demon, or maybe lucky demon? I don't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could be worse. That deadly vibrator… how angry would a demon be if one was bound into that?

There's also the joke I have about Demons and iPhones that can *really* upset Christiam Fundamentalists…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"What secret knowledge of the past or future shall I reveal to you?"
"Actually, we summoned you for your buttery radio voice."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My specific thought was that he "knows all things of the past and future".

True, though, for an audio device, clarity is the most important thing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He [Berith] speaketh with a very clear and subtle Voice.

Seems appropriate, audio clarity and subtlety are things you want from a quality sound system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I picked it because it was the only suitably demonic-looking image I was able to find in a form I could easily work with. There would be other patterns elsewhere on the chip of course.

item.title: Re: Attribution Post
item.content:encoded: Sorry, fixed now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short version: basically, they've exploited the algorithm the device uses to seek out the best speaker-quality-to-signal-strength ratio to create a warding circle, and then surrounded it with albums like The Complete Yanni and The Best of Muzak volumes 1 through 143. It has to be this exact min-maxed setup, however, or it'll start using sombody's top of the line Bose headphones on the other side of the building to play the entire memetic audio recording archives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. I recognise the Goetic Seal on the chip- but why Berith?

Excellent work though. I look forward to reading more from you!

item.title: Re: Attribution Post
item.content:encoded: No

item.title: Re: Attribution Post
item.content:encoded: Don't bloody ask me… For some reason I always thought it was written that way until you corrected me once. I think your profile should have the correct pronouncing of your name written on it.

item.title: Re: Attribution Post
item.content:encoded:
Ammuness Creeps

…

why do ppl keep spelling my nme wrng ;-;

item.title: Attribution Post
item.content:encoded:
I would like to thank a lot of different people who helped me put this together, but unfortunately can't remember most of their usernames. Please PM me if you helped so I can credit you.

In no particular order:

  • Amuness Creeps
  • Decibelles
  • TwistedGears

Image Credits:

  • SCP-2167 photo: By Andrés Galeotti from Ciudad Autónoma de Buenos Aires, Argentina [CC BY 2.0 (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0)], via Wikimedia Commons
  • SEM Imagery: My own work, released under CC-BY-SA-3.0 and 4.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure, I'll explain how it all works:

+ show block
– hide block

$p(\vec x)$ defines a scalar field that describes the spatial damping of the effect, defined as the superposition of the damping fields of each potentially affected device.

(1)
\begin{align} p(\vec x) = c_2 \sum_i \log(q_i + 1) \exp( -c_3\left\| \vec x - \vec v_i \right\| ^2) \end{align}

The most important part of this equation is the last bit:

(2)
\begin{align} \exp(- \left\| \vec x - \vec v_i \right\| ^2) \end{align}

This section is based on the equation for the PDF of the multivariate normal distribution:

(3)
\begin{align} f_{\vec x}(\vec x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^{k}|\boldsymbol\Sigma|}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}({\vec x}-{\vec\mu})^\mathrm{T}{\boldsymbol\Sigma}^{-1}({\vec x}-{\vec\mu}) \right), \end{align}

In this case I chose the covariance matrix $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ as a constant multiple of the identity matrix (making the field radially symmetric), the mean vector $\vec \mu$ to the position of the affected device, and all the constant factors stripped out and replaced with c2 and c3 which I chose by simulating various containment arrangements until it worked the way I wanted.

The purpose of all this is to make each affected device have a finite damping effect on the range of SCP-2167's effect. Using simpler laws like $1/d^n$ led to problems with speakers located directly behind a cheapo low-quality one not being affected at all due to a sort of 'shadow' cast by nearer ones.

The rest of $p(\vec x)$ is a tweak to how the IGIS value affects the devices overall damping.

The next part is actually a little simpler to understand:

(4)
\begin{align} I(\vec x) = 1 - {\left\|\vec x\right\|}\int_0^1 p(t\,\vec x)\, H\left(-\frac{\partial p(t\,\vec x)}{\partial t}\right)\; dt \end{align}

Originally when I was designing these equations I used a much simpler integral:

(5)
\begin{align} {\left\|\vec x\right\|}\int_0^1 p(t\,\vec x)\; dt \end{align}

This just calculates the total damping on a straight line path from SCP-2167 to a potentially-affected device.

However, this had a problem, in that SCP-2167's effect could be dampened completely without affecting any devices at all. As a result, I added the second term to the integral:

(6)
\begin{align} H\left(-\frac{\partial p(t\,\vec x)}{\partial t}\right) \end{align}

The Heaviside step function $H(x)$ is defined to be 1 whenever $x>0$ and 0 whenever $x<0$. $\frac{\partial p(t\,\vec x)}{\partial t}$ is the partial derivative of $p$ along the path. For those unfamiliar with partial derivatives, they are just like regular derivatives, but easier - any variable that is not itself a function of t is assumed to have a partial derivative of 0 wrt t.

So basically what this does, is that whenever p is increasing along the path (you are getting closer to a device), ignore all dampening; only count damping when p is decreasing (you are going further away). This also makes it so that a small low-quality device can't shadow a really good device close behind it, since even after passing the low-quality device, p would still be increasing due to the one behind it.

Originally when I posted this, I had had a few typos in the integration formula, but they have since been corrected to reflect what I had intended.

I wrote up a small program to test the equations, which I intend to upload once I can get it to work on my computer (originally I wrote it as an android app from my phone). Stay posted for an interactive demo of these equations at work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As much as I like the idea behind this and the ammount of work that went into it, the end result really isn't that interesting, basically being an over the top amplifier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A painful neutral vote, as I appreciate the work you've put in. The end result, IMHO, is a case of "the whole is not equal to the sum of its parts."

Still, others like it and you're safely out of deletion territory. I look forward to your future work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can someone walk me through the second and third equations, maybe with the variables inherent to the containment procedures? My vector calculus isn't really very good, I'm afraid.

item.title: Proud
item.content:encoded:
AJ, I am very impressed and kinda proud for you.

Making an article on a creative writing website is hard as shit. I really like the perseverance you had in making this. That's what gave me the upvote.

While they're is not much of a story, the fact that a demon is within a sound modifier or rather being some sound module from hell makes a good implication and I like it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't worry, there is going to be a larger story that ties in with this, which is now finished:
A Brief Explanation of Demonics

This ended up rather long, but I have addressed most of your points here.

+ show block
– hide block

As is common for SCP articles involving electronic equipment, this avoids the "it's Safe, just turn it off" argument by making it operate without power.

It doesn't merely operate without power: In the first paragraph discussing the effects if disconnected from speakers and audio, it states that "SCP-2167 also manifests both of these effects when disconnected from a power supply." Is there a better way to organize this paragraph to make this fact more prominent?

In addition, the Description notes that the amp has been disassembled and parts replaced. Does this stop the automatic anomalous effects?

No, it doesn't, and actually makes them worse. This and other aspects will be covered in a supplement I am working on entitled "A Brief Explanation of Demonics".

Even given its automatic operation, the Containment Procedures seem entirely too complex. If storing it in a disassembled state won't work, has a Faraday cage been tried?

Was tried, didn't work; effect does not depend on EM transmission.

And why use a CD player with classical music/easy-listening? That seems like too many mechanical components that could break (as well as music that could vary in volume too much) - why not a radio or an electronic feed?

When it was first contained in the 1970s containment used a vinyl record player, due to a perception that vinyl records were more stable to the influences of anomalous things than magnetic tapes, but there were always issues with various parts of record player and the vinyl records themselves wearing out, requiring frequent maintenance.

Containment was modified to use audio CDs when they became available in the 80s, reducing maintenance costs significantly. When computerized audio players became available, it was decided to continue using CD media, again because it was perceived as more stable than flash or magnetic hard drive storage. Additionally, audio CDs have a very high IGIS value, so their containment potential in case the CD player fails is fairly good.

The reason that there are still vinyl records stored alongside SCP-2167 is a relic from the switchover to using CDs, when the reliability of the technology was not fully confirmed. Thus it was decided that some vinyl records should be kept to assist in containment, in case a previously-unknown type of degradation occurred with the CDs rendering them unable to fully contain SCP-2167.

You wouldn't even need a signal - you could amplify static or a single tone

(Note, I am editing it to just be classical music.)

But that doesn't fit what I had in mind for it. It just doesn't. Having classical music blasting throughout the facility, with 682 smashing down walls and massive explosions in the background, is a hundred times better than the same scene with static or a single deafening tone.

For some reason the idea of a chair in the testing suite really irritated me

The chair is specified as part of the golden cuboid arrangement - any testing needs to be done under standard acoustic conditions to be reliable, and that includes the position of the test subject relative to the speakers and room.

couldn't you have tests in the sound-proof locker (which presumably should also be a golden cuboid)?

I would assume that the locker used would not be of an appropriate size to conduct testing. I imagine it as stored in just one small locker of many in a large storage area. This is actually a cost-cutting measure, originally it was just contained in the test chamber but it was determined to be of low-enough risk that it could be stored long-term in a containment locker.

The first two paragraphs of the description don't tell us what the anomaly is, and spend a lot of time covering details that could be assimilated by the reader based on the picture, and the sentence "SCP-2167 is a "Gauchito-78" audio amplifier."

You are assuming that every reader is able to see the image, while this may in fact not be true, whether from physical impairment or technological limitations of the system being used to view it. In general the text ought to be able to stand on its own, and the images serve only as supplementary material. (The only reason I didn't provide a description of the SEM imagery is because it depends on iconography that would be meaningless to a blind person anyway.)

The final addendum left more questions than it answered. If this was being used in a private residence, how did the Foundation learn of its existence? More to the point, how did the Foundation learn of its existence in a way that didn't involve a public incident worth mentioning in this report? And what's with the stray "W" left unredacted in the name?

I had a recovery log in an earlier version, but it was recommended that I cut it because it didn't add much. But to summarize: Mr. █████ W██████ was acquitted of charges (disorderly conduct, disturbing the peace, interfering with an officer of the law, reckless endangerment, etc.) resulting from his using SCP-2167 in a music concert, based on sound pressure measurements at the event in question. This led to local papers running stories about it, the Foundation catching wind of something amok and investigating.

The reason for the unredacted "W" was because his name was used multiple times in the earlier version. You can't identify someone by just the first letter of their last name so it isn't a security breach.

And the final reference to "infinity and beyond" was confusing. Is it anachronistic (which was my interpretation based on the look of the amp and the reference to '78, although there is no information on when this was made) or did the maker of the amp steal the phrase from Pixar?

If you look at this google ngrams plot, the phrase "to infinity and beyond" surfaced in 1933, well before this device's supposed date of manufacture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading through the very detailed Containment Procedures and the opening of the Description, I was prepared to give this the benefit of the doubt, despite a few issues (see bullet points below). I was fine with the formulae, although I have no way to distinguish real from made-up functions, so they didn't add a lot of meaning or interest. But seeing the level of detail set me up to expect a very long article, with a really juicy story to justify the build-up. I liked where the "seal of Solomon" and the SEM picture were going, and was hopeful about a larger story just around the corner.

Then you dropped a two sentence paragraph using the phrases "demon effect", "demon particle" and "demon gate", and saying that this was incompletely understood. That, even more than the sum of the smaller issues, was what killed this for me. This article suggests that, in-universe, Foundation personnel need external links to know what IGIS is, a paragraph to explain the "gain" and "tone" knobs of an amplifier, and would benefit from detailed exploration of the maths and electronics involved, but don't require any explanation as to the role of demons in electrical equipment. And as a piece of fiction, you have spent 20-odd paragraphs on background details, only to dismiss the interesting core idea (which involves a huge amount of world-building) without turning it into a story.

+ Other issues
- None of these fatal on their own
  • The basic idea here is the intersection of two very common tropes on the site: an item that performs its function anomalously well, and something that makes very loud sounds. Common tropes are fine, but they carry a weight of history that needs to be overcome to succeed.
  • As is common for SCP articles involving electronic equipment, this avoids the "it's Safe, just turn it off" argument by making it operate without power. However the nature of this item means that this one needs to go further - not only does it operate without power, it also operates without connections to speakers or audio sources. Why? If it is designed to operate without power, why have a power connection at all? You say in the PL documentation at the end that connecting it gives "superior performance" but that isn't evident from the Description. And nothing in the article explains why (in-universe) it has an automatic wireless connection with speakers, or why it selects media remotely (or how it selects, what constitutes audio recordings, or what "nearby" means in this context). It doesn't help that this paragraph turns up half-way through the Description, meaning that the question about disconnecting it is left hanging (and is magnified by the second paragraph stating that the item functions as an amplifier "when connected to a set of speakers, a power supply and an audio source").
  • In addition, the Description notes that the amp has been disassembled and parts replaced. Does this stop the automatic anomalous effects?
  • Even given its automatic operation, the Containment Procedures seem entirely too complex. If storing it in a disassembled state won't work, has a Faraday cage been tried? - that would stop a range of signals from the amp to external speakers/sound sources. Why connect it to a power source at all? If it's going to operate regardless, it seems a waste of electricity to connect it up. And why use a CD player with classical music/easy-listening? That seems like too many mechanical components that could break (as well as music that could vary in volume too much) - why not a radio or an electronic feed? You wouldn't even need a signal - you could amplify static or a single tone. And why are vinyl records needed at all? And if testing is required, why take the risk of moving it to a separate room - couldn't you have tests in the sound-proof locker (which presumably should also be a golden cuboid)? For some reason the idea of a chair in the testing suite really irritated me - it was the final example of why all this detail was unnecessary both in-universe and out.
  • The first two paragraphs of the description don't tell us what the anomaly is, and spend a lot of time covering details that could be assimilated by the reader based on the picture, and the sentence "SCP-2167 is a "Gauchito-78" audio amplifier."
  • The final addendum left more questions than it answered. If this was being used in a private residence, how did the Foundation learn of its existence? More to the point, how did the Foundation learn of its existence in a way that didn't involve a public incident worth mentioning in this report? And what's with the stray "W" left unredacted in the name?
  • The note itself didn't add any new information to the Description - just re-hashed the same information in marketing terms. And the final reference to "infinity and beyond" was confusing. Is it anachronistic (which was my interpretation based on the look of the amp and the reference to '78, although there is no information on when this was made) or did the maker of the amp steal the phrase from Pixar? Neither explanation makes a lot of sense, or seems to lead anywhere narratively.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had me at $I(\vec x) = 1 - \int_0^1 p(t\,\vec x)\, H\left(\frac{\partial p(t\,\vec x)}{\partial t}\right)\; dt$

Applied demonology. This is headcanon now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: sones are indeed not part of the SI unit system. This is of necessity, as there are no units for psychoacoustic loudness in the SI unit system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't that a non-SI unit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I guess I need to make that clear. S is in sones. Edited to incorporate.
Also, I am aware how quickly the ackermann function grows. But at m=4 it is still at a point where the foundation could theoretically measure it using psychoacoustic tests.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What units is Sin in? If that is supposed to be decibel scale, it very quickly becomes ridiculous (and stops begin perceptible as sound at all). The Ackermann function grows obscenely huge very quickly and decibels are a logarithmic scale…

Sout (3,60)= 576,460,752,303,423,485 dB which is a meaninglessly huge measure of perceived loudness (and 60 dB is quiet for a stereo).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It isn't an -Ex since we don't know why or how its effects happen, and is still too dangerous to stop containment. But perhaps at T + 2-3 years they will have figured it out completely.

With respect to the variable subscripts, there is no pun. Sin is the input loudness, and Sout is the output loudness.

As for your last question, the supplementary tale a brief explanation on demonics explains it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ extensive remarks
- hide text

Good writing means hitting a moving target. For mainlist articles, an essential quality is hitting that clinical, detached feeling that we get by filtering the unreal through the lens of scientific inquiry. That means striving for the clinical tone that everyone is always citing but rarely elucidating. Writing a mainlist article means putting enough scientific detail and accuracy into something to make it believable as a scientific report to the reader.

The other side of that is that scientific literature is boring as shit1. When you stuff in too much scientific detail, you oversell the piece, and instead of increasing the reader's immersion, you add uninteresting filler into an article and dilute the good parts, to the point where hunting for narrative becomes not worth the effort. The mainlist article must be just scientific enough to aid the reading experience.

This article is overselling the scientific angle. I get that the exceedingly (by the standards of fiction) complex data and observations are the raison d'etre of the piece. The problem with that is that purely scientific writeups generally make for boring reading, and in this case, I couldn't care less about any of the equations or the specific measurements of the thing or the circuitry or any of that. All of that stuff is so far beyond my experience as a layperson that you could have completely made up large swathes of it, and I (and I suspect everyone else aside from like one or two people) wouldn't be able to tell the difference. That's not really adding immersion.

The rest of the article does nothing to save it, either. "Thing what gets really really loud" is the heart of the article, supposedly, and I'm simply unmoved by that as a premise. As an aside, I'm not sure why we're perpetually seeing really loud things around here, but the fact that I'm fairly tired of seeing those kinds of things isn't helping. The Prometheus Labs angle doesn't do anything to advance the narrative, aside from a pat explanation and an opportunity to have some convenient explanatory literature lying around.

The argument could be made that in-universe, the Foundation would have lots of boring documents like this lying around. And that is factually correct. The Foundation would also have shipping manifests for the month's order of paper towels for Site-19, copies of the services contracts for their landscaping at several covert facilities, requisition forms for safety gear in the Euclid wing, and any number of things that nobody wants to actually read. Verisimilitude is not the same as narrative interest.

Thankfully, the ill-advised supplement was deleted, which gave this an undercurrent of mean-spiritedness in addition to the article's other problems.

Footnotes
1. Inevitably, someone is going to say "but academic journals relevant to my field aren't boring," in which case you may amend my statement to "boring as shit to everyone except you."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The technical writing here is amazing, but well, it's just not really that interesting an object, imo. :/ I wish by the end of it all I was invested in…something besides a wonky amplifier. Maybe there's more here to latch onto, but I missed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why these puns with "Soul" and Sin? What exactly is the "demon particle"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The suppliment is done now! A Brief Explanation of Demonics

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I am a sucker for articles like these, it doesn't really stand without the supplements you showed me in chat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I almost downvoted because, well, it's an amp that's just really good at being an amp. Looking at the The Big List of Cliches, we see that comes in at #11. [Crank it up to 11!] There's just not much of a hook, and the Demon thing feels a little tacked-on.

I upvoted because its obvious you put a metric shit-tonne of work into this thing, and, as a technical writer myself, its probably the most technically detailed article I've seen on the wiki. And that made it feel real. It's a testament to clinical tone and attention to detail. I probably wouldn't read it again, but I think it is something that can belong on the site and flesh out the Foundation universe a little bit better. It feels like something the Foundation would actually have in their database files somewhere. Again, it feels real.
